lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 9 - Attention, Final Projects and LLM Intro

unit.name: Pretrained LLMs and Practical Project Design
unit.description: Places current project design in the era of large pretrained models, emphasizing practicality, lightweight adaptation, API usage, and research questions that go beyond simply calling a model.
unit.summary: The closing segment argues that the research landscape has shifted: earlier projects often tried to invent better architectures from scratch, but most modern work builds on large pretrained models whose architecture is largely fixed [ts=4280s] [ts=4377s]. For most practical projects, the lecturer recommends loading pretrained models—especially via Hugging Face—and adding analysis, adaptation, or surrounding system logic rather than reinventing the base network [ts=4399s] [ts=4428s]. He warns students to stay realistic about scale because many flashy papers rely on compute far beyond course budgets, which is why lightweight methods such as parameter-efficient fine-tuning are attractive [ts=4463s] [ts=4534s]. Finally, he explicitly permits API-based use of models like GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, and LLaMA-class systems, while insisting that a strong project must ask an interesting research question—such as prompting, in-context learning, bias analysis, or reasoning behavior—rather than merely piping documents through a model and stopping there [ts=4550s] [ts=4653s].

key_points.text: Modern projects usually build on large pretrained models rather than inventing new full architectures.
For many practical NLP projects, loading a pretrained model and building analysis or adaptation around it is the sensible default.
Students should prefer practical, lightweight methods such as parameter-efficient fine-tuning under limited compute.
Using commercial or frontier models is acceptable when done through API access.
A project is not complete just because a strong model produced good summaries; the research contribution must come from the surrounding question and analysis.

For most practical projects, the lecturer recommends loading pretrained models—especially via Hugging Face—and adding analysis, adaptation, or surrounding system logic rather than reinventing the base network [ts=4399s] [ts=4428s]. He warns students to stay realistic about scale because many flashy papers rely on compute far beyond course budgets, which is why lightweight methods such as parameter-efficient fine-tuning are attractive [ts=4463s] [ts=4534s]. Finally, he explicitly permits API-based use of models like GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, and LLaMA-class systems, while insisting that a strong project must ask an interesting research question—such as prompting, in-context learning, bias analysis, or reasoning behavior—rather than merely piping documents through a model and stopping there [ts=4550s] [ts=4653s].

kp_catalog.name: LLM-era project strategy
kp_catalog.description: Modern course projects often build on pretrained LLMs using PEFT, in-context learning, or API-driven experimentation, but still need a genuine research question and analysis.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because lLM-era project strategy clarifies modern course projects often build on pretrained LLMs using PEFT, in-context learning, or API-driven experimentation, but still need a genuine research question and analysis, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Compute-aware project planning
kp_catalog.description: Project design should respect realistic GPU and API budgets and favor lightweight methods when full-scale training is infeasible.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because compute-aware project planning clarifies project design should respect realistic GPU and API budgets and favor lightweight methods when full-scale training is infeasible, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Families of viable project types
kp_catalog.description: CS224N projects can be task-driven, method-driven, interpretability-oriented, theoretical, or reimplementation-based, provided they remain substantive and evaluable.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because families of viable project types clarifies cS224N projects can be task-driven, method-driven, interpretability-oriented, theoretical, or reimplementation-based, provided they remain substantive and evaluable, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
